info.name: Apache Log4j2 Remote Code Execution (Log4Shell)
info.description: Apache Log4j2 versions 2.0-beta9 through 2.15.0 are vulnerable to remote code execution via JNDI lookups in log messages. An unauthenticated attacker who can cause a logged string to be processed can execute arbitrary code on the server.

info.name: Apache Struts2 Jakarta Multipart Parser RCE
info.description: The Jakarta Multipart parser in Apache Struts 2.3.x before 2.3.32 and 2.5.x before 2.5.10.1 mishandles the Content-Type header, allowing remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands via a crafted Content-Type, Content-Disposition, or Content-Length HTTP header.

info.name: Apache HTTP Server 2.4.17-2.4.38 Local Privilege Escalation
info.description: In Apache HTTP Server 2.4 releases 2.4.17 to 2.4.38, with MPM event, worker or prefork, code executing in less-privileged child processes or threads could execute arbitrary code with the privileges of the parent process by manipulating the scoreboard.

info.name: SQL Injection - Error Based
info.description: The application returned a database error when a single quote was injected into a request parameter, indicating that user input is concatenated into a SQL query without proper parameterization. This can allow extraction or modification of database contents.

extracted-results: You have an error in your SQL syntax near '''

info.name: phpMyAdmin Panel - Exposed
info.description: A publicly accessible phpMyAdmin administrative interface was discovered. Exposed database administration panels are a frequent target for credential-stuffing and known phpMyAdmin vulnerabilities, and can lead to full database compromise.

info.name: Git Configuration - Exposure
info.description: A .git/config file is publicly accessible. This exposes the repository structure and remote URLs, and frequently allows an attacker to reconstruct application source code — potentially including hard-coded credentials and secrets.

info.name: CORS Misconfiguration - Reflected Origin
info.description: The application reflects an arbitrary Origin header into the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header while also setting Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. This allows any origin to read authenticated responses, enabling cross-origin theft of sensitive data.

info.name: HTTP Missing Security Headers
info.description: The HTTP response is missing one or more recommended security headers, including Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, and X-Frame-Options. These headers provide defence-in-depth against clickjacking, protocol downgrade, and content-injection attacks.

info.name: Directory Listing Enabled
info.description: The web server returned an automatically generated directory index, exposing the names of files that are not intended to be browsable. This can reveal backups, configuration files, and other sensitive artifacts.

info.name: Public Swagger API - Detect
info.description: A publicly accessible Swagger/OpenAPI definition was found. Exposed API documentation can reveal internal endpoints, parameters, and authentication schemes useful to an attacker mapping the application.

info.name: Deprecated TLS 1.0 Supported
info.description: The server negotiates TLS 1.0, a deprecated protocol with known cryptographic weaknesses (BEAST, POODLE). Modern clients and compliance frameworks (PCI DSS) require TLS 1.2 or higher.

info.name: Cookies without Secure attribute - Detect
info.description: One or more cookies are set without the Secure attribute, allowing them to be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections where they can be intercepted.

info.name: Cookies without HttpOnly attribute - Detect
info.description: One or more cookies are set without the HttpOnly attribute, making them readable by client-side JavaScript and increasing the impact of any cross-site scripting vulnerability.

info.name: Apache HTTP Server - Detect
info.description: Detected the Apache HTTP Server software and version from response headers.

info.name: WAF Detection
info.description: A Web Application Firewall was fingerprinted in front of the target. WAF presence is useful context for tuning subsequent testing.
